definition_short: Vertical lines and tall elements dominate the space, emphasizing height and upward direction.
definition_long: Use when vertical elements such as columns, tall walls, or elongated features are the primary visual cue. The composition should feel oriented upward with strong vertical alignment. Apply to spaces where vertical directionality shapes perception, not merely where a few tall items appear. Do not use when horizontality or diagonal features dominate. This tag is about directional emphasis rather than ceiling height alone.
examples_positive: A hall with tall columns that define the space.
A lobby with floor-to-ceiling vertical fins.
examples_negative: A space dominated by horizontal bands.
A room with mostly low, wide furniture.
extraction_notes_2d: Detect line orientations and confirm a high proportion of near-vertical edges. Look for repeated vertical elements across the frame. Ensure verticality is a dominant compositional cue, not a minor detail. Avoid tagging when vertical lines are present but overwhelmed by horizontal structure.
extraction_notes_3d: Analyze edge direction statistics and surface normals for vertical alignment. Tag when vertical edges significantly exceed other orientations. Use object height ratios to confirm tall, upright elements in the main view.

definition_short: Security or surveillance cameras are visible in the scene.
definition_long: Use when cameras or related surveillance hardware are clearly identifiable and visible. This includes dome cameras, bullet cameras, or camera clusters mounted on walls or ceilings. Apply when camera devices are present and recognizable. Do not use for generic sensors or speakers unless clearly cameras. The tag is about visible surveillance hardware.
examples_positive: A ceiling with a visible dome camera near the entrance.
A wall-mounted bullet camera overlooking a corridor.
examples_negative: A motion sensor without a camera lens.
A speaker grille that resembles a camera.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for camera housings with lenses and mounting brackets, often near ceilings or entrances. Use object detection for CCTV or camera shapes. Confirm a lens or dome form to avoid confusion with speakers. Consider typical placement patterns for surveillance devices.
extraction_notes_3d: Use object metadata for cameras and their mount points. Tag when surveillance camera objects are visible in the primary field of view. Check orientation and placement near access points for additional confirmation.

definition_short: Large amounts of visible text or information are present, creating a dense informational field.
definition_long: Use when the scene contains many text elements or densely packed informational displays. This includes signage clusters, wall graphics with extensive text, or multiple posters and notices. Apply when text density is visually dominant. Do not use for a single sign or sparse labeling. This tag is about text volume, not branding alone.
examples_positive: A bulletin board covered with many notices and flyers.
A transit wall with multiple schedules and maps.
examples_negative: A single exit sign on an otherwise blank wall.
A logo sign with minimal text.
extraction_notes_2d: Use OCR or text detection to count text regions and measure area coverage. High density shows many text boxes across the frame. Confirm that text is spatially distributed and visually dominant. Avoid tagging if text is small, distant, or limited to one corner.
extraction_notes_3d: Use scene graph labels for sign objects and text textures. Compute text area coverage in the view. Tag when text objects exceed a density threshold across main surfaces.

definition_short: Lighting is even and consistent across the space with minimal hotspots or shadows.
definition_long: Use when illumination appears evenly distributed, with similar brightness across surfaces and few strong shadows. The space should feel uniformly lit rather than dramatic or spotlit. Apply to offices, retail, or rooms with diffuse ambient lighting. Do not use when lighting contrast is high or uneven. This tag emphasizes lighting uniformity.
examples_positive: An office with evenly lit ceiling panels throughout.
A store with uniform ambient lighting across aisles.
examples_negative: A gallery with strong spotlights and dark corners.
A room with bright window patches and deep shadows.
extraction_notes_2d: Measure luminance variance across the frame and check for low contrast between regions. Uniform lighting shows a narrow brightness distribution. Confirm that shadows and hotspots are minimal. Avoid tagging if daylight creates strong gradients.
extraction_notes_3d: Use illuminance sampling to compute variance across the floor and walls. Tag when illuminance is relatively even and contrast ratios are low. Identify absence of strong directional light gradients.

definition_short: Windows are clearly visible as openings or glazed elements in the scene.
definition_long: Use when windows are present and identifiable, regardless of size or openness. The tag indicates visible window elements such as frames, panes, or glazing. Apply even if the windows are small or closed. Do not use if no window elements are visible. This tag is about visibility, not area.
examples_positive: A room with multiple windows along one wall.
A corridor with a window at the end.
examples_negative: A windowless interior room.
A space with only interior glass partitions and no windows.
extraction_notes_2d: Detect window frames and glazed areas, often with views or brightness differences. Use segmentation for window regions. Confirm window features rather than mirrors or glass doors. Avoid tagging if glass is purely internal partitioning.
extraction_notes_3d: Use scene graph to identify window objects and their visibility in the camera frustum. Tag when window meshes are within view. Distinguish windows from glass partitions by checking exterior adjacency.

definition_short: Wood is a dominant visible material in the scene.
definition_long: Use when wood surfaces cover a substantial portion of the visible area and strongly influence the material impression. This includes wood floors, wall panels, ceilings, or large furniture pieces. Apply when wood reads as a primary finish rather than minor accents. Do not use for small wooden items only. The tag focuses on material prominence.
examples_positive: A space with wood floors and wood wall paneling.
A room dominated by timber beams and wood ceiling.
examples_negative: A room with only a small wooden table.
A concrete space with minor wood trim.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for wood grain textures and warm brown hues across large surfaces. Confirm that wood covers a significant area, not just small objects. Use material classification to distinguish wood from wood-look finishes when possible. Avoid tagging if wood is limited to trim.
extraction_notes_3d: Use material metadata to identify wood surfaces and compute visible area proportion. Tag when wood surfaces exceed a threshold of the view. Combine with texture cues such as grain patterns to validate.

definition_short: Natural materials such as wood and stone are prominently visible in the space.
definition_long: Use when the scene features substantial areas of natural wood and stone finishes, giving an organic material character. Both materials should be visible and notable, not just one small accent. Apply when wood and stone are primary materials for floors, walls, or major elements. Do not use when surfaces are synthetic imitations or only one material is present. This tag is about natural material presence.
examples_positive: A lobby with a stone floor and wood wall paneling.
A space with a stone feature wall and timber ceiling beams.
examples_negative: A room with wood furniture but no stone surfaces.
A space with stone tile but no visible wood.
extraction_notes_2d: Detect wood grain and stone textures on large surfaces. Confirm presence of both materials within the same scene. Use material classifiers to reduce confusion with printed or synthetic finishes. Avoid tagging if one material is only a small accent.
extraction_notes_3d: Use material tags for wood and stone and compute visible area coverage for each. Tag when both materials exceed minimum coverage thresholds. Validate with surface texture properties and reflectance characteristics.

definition_short: Visible hazard warnings or safety indicators are present in the space.
definition_long: Use when safety signage, warning labels, hazard tape, or caution markings are clearly visible. The indicators should communicate risk or safety instructions, such as caution signs, wet floor warnings, or hazard stripes. Apply when these indicators are noticeable within the scene. Do not use for generic informational signs without hazard content. This tag is about safety signaling.
examples_positive: A wet floor caution sign in a corridor.
A doorway marked with hazard stripes and warning labels.
examples_negative: A room with standard directional signage only.
A decorative stripe pattern without warning meaning.
extraction_notes_2d: Use text and symbol detection to identify warning words and hazard icons. Yellow-black stripes and caution colors are common cues. Confirm semantic content indicates hazard or safety. Avoid tagging on decorative striping without warning context.
extraction_notes_3d: Use object labels for safety signs and hazard markers. Tag when hazard-related objects are visible in the view. Combine with OCR or icon classification to validate warning content.

definition_short: The space appears cluttered with many objects, leaving little clear surface area.
definition_long: Use when objects are densely packed, creating a visually busy and cluttered environment. The clutter can be from furniture, merchandise, or miscellaneous items. Apply when clutter reduces clear surfaces or pathways. Do not use for spaces that are orderly despite having many objects. This tag focuses on clutter level.
examples_positive: A storage room filled with stacked boxes and items.
A shop with merchandise covering most surfaces.
examples_negative: A minimal room with a few pieces of furniture.
A neatly organized shelf display with clear spacing.
extraction_notes_2d: Estimate object count and free surface area. High clutter shows many overlapping objects and few clear regions. Distinguish clutter from high seating density by looking at mixed object types. Avoid tagging if the scene is dense but highly organized.
extraction_notes_3d: Compute object density per area and occlusion rate. Tag when navigable free space is reduced by object volume. Use scene graph clutter metrics if available.

definition_short: Overall lighting levels are very bright, creating a high-illuminance environment.
definition_long: Use when the scene appears brightly lit with high overall light levels. The key cue is strong brightness across large areas, not just localized highlights. Apply to spaces with intense artificial lighting or strong daylight that lifts overall luminance. Do not use for normal or dim lighting. This tag indicates high light levels.
examples_positive: A retail floor with very bright, evenly lit aisles.
A room flooded with daylight from large windows.
examples_negative: A softly lit lounge.
A dim corridor with low lighting.
extraction_notes_2d: Measure average luminance and ensure it is high across the frame. Confirm brightness is not limited to small hotspots. Use exposure cues and histogram distribution to gauge overall light level. Avoid tagging when brightness is due to overexposure in a small area.
extraction_notes_3d: Use illuminance estimates or lighting simulation to compute average lux across the space. Tag when mean illuminance exceeds a defined high threshold. Verify that bright levels are spatially widespread.

definition_short: Objects and elements are arranged in a highly orderly and systematic manner.
definition_long: Use when the layout shows strong alignment, consistent spacing, and clear organization. The space should feel structured and disciplined rather than casual or messy. Apply to rows of chairs, aligned furniture, or neatly arranged displays. Do not use for eclectic or irregular arrangements. This tag focuses on organizational order.
examples_positive: Chairs arranged in evenly spaced rows.
A retail display with consistent alignment and spacing.
examples_negative: Furniture placed haphazardly.
A cluttered room with uneven spacing.
extraction_notes_2d: Check for alignment along common axes and consistent spacing between objects. High orderliness yields strong regularity in object placement. Use line detection and object centroid grids to quantify. Avoid tagging if only a small area is ordered.
extraction_notes_3d: Compute alignment and spacing variance across object clusters. Tag when variance is low and arrangement is grid-like or row-based. Use layout regularity metrics to confirm.

definition_short: Live plants are visible inside the space.
definition_long: Use when potted plants, planters, or indoor vegetation are clearly visible. The plants should be real and intentional, not just printed imagery. Apply when plants are a noticeable element of the interior. Do not use for outdoor vegetation seen through windows or plant imagery. This tag is about real indoor plants.
examples_positive: A lobby with large potted plants near seating.
An office with planters along the walls.
examples_negative: A mural of a forest.
Trees visible only outside through windows.
extraction_notes_2d: Detect plant shapes, leaves, and planters within the interior. Confirm that plants are inside the space rather than outside views. Distinguish real plants from images or patterns. Use object detection for plants and planters.
extraction_notes_3d: Use object categories for vegetation and check spatial location within interior zones. Tag when live plant objects are visible and not behind exterior boundaries. Consider scale and placement in the scene graph.

definition_short: Overall lighting levels are low, resulting in a dimly lit environment.
definition_long: Use when the scene appears generally dark with low ambient light levels. The cue is low overall luminance rather than localized shadows. Apply to dim interiors, night scenes, or minimally lit spaces. Do not use for normal or bright lighting. This tag indicates low light levels.
examples_positive: A lounge lit only by a few small lamps.
A corridor with very low ambient light.
examples_negative: A brightly lit office.
A space with strong daylight.
extraction_notes_2d: Measure average luminance and ensure it is low across most of the frame. Confirm that darkness is not limited to corners while the rest is bright. Use exposure histograms to assess overall light level. Avoid tagging when low light is due to a small shadowed area.
extraction_notes_3d: Use illuminance estimates to compute average lux values. Tag when mean illuminance falls below a defined threshold for low light. Confirm that low levels are spatially widespread.

definition_short: The palette is minimal or monochrome, with very few hues dominating the scene.
definition_long: Use when the space is characterized by a single dominant color or a very limited set of hues. The palette should feel restrained and minimal rather than varied. Apply to monochrome or near-monochrome interiors with little color variation. Do not use when multiple distinct hues are present. This tag is about palette simplicity.
examples_positive: A white-on-white gallery space with minimal color variation.
A room dominated by grey tones with no strong accents.
examples_negative: A room with multiple bold colors.
A space with varied decor hues.
extraction_notes_2d: Compute hue histogram and verify a narrow distribution with few peaks. Monochrome palettes show low hue variance even if brightness varies. Confirm that accents are minimal and not dominant. Avoid tagging when small but vivid accents significantly change the palette.
extraction_notes_3d: Aggregate material colors and compute hue variance across major surfaces. Tag when hue variance is low and one hue dominates. Consider lighting that shifts perceived hue across surfaces.

definition_short: Forms mimic organic, nature-inspired shapes rather than strict geometry.
definition_long: Use when architectural or design elements feature organic, flowing, or biomorphic shapes. The forms should evoke natural patterns like curves, branching, or irregular organic contours. Apply when these shapes are prominent in the space. Do not use for purely geometric curves or standard rectilinear forms. This tag is about nature-inspired form language.
examples_positive: A ceiling with branching, tree-like structural forms.
Furniture with smooth, organic contours inspired by nature.
examples_negative: A room with only straight, rectilinear walls.
A space with simple circular columns but no organic cues.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for flowing contours and irregular organic shapes that resemble natural forms. Use shape descriptors to detect non-orthogonal, non-circular organic outlines. Confirm that naturalistic shapes are dominant, not minor accents. Avoid tagging on generic curves without organic cues.
extraction_notes_3d: Analyze surface curvature and form descriptors for organic, biomorphic shapes. Tag when a significant portion of geometry deviates from simple primitives and resembles natural forms. Use clustering of curvature patterns to validate.

definition_short: Religious symbols or iconography are visible in the space.
definition_long: Use when clearly identifiable religious symbols, objects, or iconography are present, such as crosses, crescents, statues, or altars. The symbols should be intentional and visible. Apply to worship spaces or rooms with religious decor. Do not use for ambiguous shapes without clear religious meaning. This tag is about visible religious iconography.
examples_positive: A church interior with a prominent cross on the wall.
A prayer room with visible religious symbols and artifacts.
examples_negative: Abstract art with no religious motifs.
A room with generic geometric decor.
extraction_notes_2d: Use symbol detection and context cues to identify religious icons. Look for recognizable shapes and associated artifacts. Confirm that symbols are intentional and central enough to notice. Avoid tagging when shapes are ambiguous or purely decorative.
extraction_notes_3d: Use object labels for religious artifacts and iconography. Tag when such objects are visible in the main view. Consider placement such as altars or shrines to reinforce classification.

definition_short: Soft acoustic treatments such as panels, baffles, or absorptive materials are visible.
definition_long: Use when acoustic treatments designed to absorb sound are visible, including fabric panels, ceiling baffles, or acoustic clouds. The treatments should be identifiable as acoustic elements, not just decorative fabric. Apply to offices, studios, or auditoriums with visible sound-absorbing features. Do not use for general textiles unless clearly acoustic. This tag focuses on acoustic treatment presence.
examples_positive: A ceiling with hanging acoustic baffles.
Walls lined with fabric acoustic panels.
examples_negative: Curtains used purely for decoration.
A room with no visible acoustic elements.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for panel arrays, baffles, or ceiling clouds with fabric or perforated surfaces. Acoustic elements often appear in regular patterns or suspended systems. Confirm context like studios or offices with visible sound treatments. Avoid tagging simple drapery unless clearly acoustic.
extraction_notes_3d: Use object metadata for acoustic panels and baffles. Tag when absorptive elements are visible and cover significant area. Consider placement on walls or ceilings consistent with acoustic treatment.

definition_short: Seating is predominantly soft or upholstered, emphasizing cushioning and comfort.
definition_long: Use when most seating appears cushioned or upholstered, such as sofas, padded chairs, or lounge seating. The cue is softness of seating surfaces, not just their presence. Apply when soft seating dominates the scene. Do not use when seating is mostly hard or unpadded. This tag contrasts with hard_seating.
examples_positive: A lounge with sofas and padded armchairs.
A waiting area with cushioned seats.
examples_negative: A cafeteria with molded plastic chairs.
A space with metal benches only.
extraction_notes_2d: Detect seating objects and assess upholstery cues such as fabric textures and thick cushions. Soft seating often has rounded forms and visible padding. Apply when soft seating is the dominant seating type. Avoid tagging when only a few soft seats are present among many hard seats.
extraction_notes_3d: Use material properties and mesh shapes to classify seating as soft. Compute proportion of seating objects with soft materials. Tag when soft seating dominates seating count or area.

definition_short: Stone or marble surfaces are a dominant visible material in the scene.
definition_long: Use when stone or marble finishes cover a large portion of visible surfaces, such as floors, walls, or columns. The material should read clearly as stone or marble through texture and veining. Apply when stone is a primary finish, not just small accents. Do not use for synthetic imitations unless clearly stone. This tag is about material prominence.
examples_positive: A lobby with marble floors and stone walls.
A hall with large stone columns.
examples_negative: A room with only a small marble countertop.
A space with painted surfaces that mimic stone.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for stone textures, veining, and characteristic reflectance on large surfaces. Confirm that stone covers a significant area in view. Distinguish from concrete or tile when possible by texture cues. Avoid tagging if stone is only a small feature.
extraction_notes_3d: Use material tags for stone or marble and compute visible area coverage. Tag when stone surfaces exceed a threshold. Validate with texture properties such as veining patterns and roughness.

definition_short: The scene provides a visible view to the outdoors through windows or openings.
definition_long: Use when the exterior environment is clearly visible through windows, doors, or open facades. The view should show outdoor elements such as sky, vegetation, or buildings. Apply even if the opening is closed, as long as the outdoor view is visible. Do not use for interior-only views or opaque glazing. This tag focuses on visual connection to outside.
examples_positive: A room with windows showing trees and sky.
A lobby with glass doors revealing the outside street.
examples_negative: A windowless interior room.
A space with frosted glass that blocks the view.
extraction_notes_2d: Detect window regions and confirm visible outdoor content beyond them. Use scene classification to identify sky, vegetation, or exterior structures. Avoid tagging when glazing is opaque or reflective without a view. Ensure the outdoor view is not a screen or image.
extraction_notes_3d: Use scene graph adjacency to exterior zones and check visibility through openings. Tag when the camera has a line of sight to an exterior environment. Confirm that the view is to outside geometry, not a display texture.

definition_short: Visible dirt, grime, or decay is present on surfaces or objects.
definition_long: Use when dirt, stains, mold, rust, or decay is clearly visible and affects the appearance of the space. The signs should be noticeable and not just minor wear. Apply to neglected or deteriorated environments. Do not use for clean or well maintained spaces. This tag focuses on visible degradation and grime.
examples_positive: Walls with visible grime and staining.
A space with rusted fixtures and decayed surfaces.
examples_negative: A clean room with no visible stains.
A well maintained lobby.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for discoloration, stains, rust, or patchy surfaces. Use texture analysis to detect grime and irregular surface patterns. Confirm that dirt is visible across meaningful areas. Avoid tagging when dirt is minimal or ambiguous.
extraction_notes_3d: Use material condition layers or defect detection to identify dirt and decay. Tag when defect density exceeds a threshold on visible surfaces. Consider both structural elements and furnishings.

definition_short: The overall palette is dominated by warm hues such as reds, oranges, and yellows.
definition_long: Use when the dominant colors are warm and create a warm visual impression. This is about overall palette, not just lighting temperature. Apply when warm colors cover large surfaces or decor. Do not use if the palette is balanced or cool. This tag is about hue dominance.
examples_positive: A room with terracotta walls and warm wood tones.
A space with orange and red decor as the main colors.
examples_negative: A blue-green dominated space.
A neutral grey and white room.
extraction_notes_2d: Compute hue distribution and verify a dominance of warm hue ranges. Confirm that warm colors are present across large areas, not just accents. Avoid tagging when warm lighting casts warm tone but surfaces are cool. Use color segmentation for major surfaces.
extraction_notes_3d: Aggregate material colors and compute warm hue proportion. Tag when warm hues exceed a threshold across visible surfaces. Consider lighting influence separately from material color.

definition_short: Lighting appears warm, with a yellow or amber color temperature.
definition_long: Use when illumination has a warm cast, typical of incandescent or warm LED lighting. Whites and neutrals should appear slightly yellow or amber. Apply when the lighting temperature is clearly warm across the scene. Do not use when lighting is cool or neutral. This tag concerns light color, not surface color.
examples_positive: A restaurant lit by warm pendant lights with amber glow.
A living room with warm lamps casting yellow light.
examples_negative: A corridor lit by cool white fluorescent lights.
Daylight with neutral or cool cast.
extraction_notes_2d: Estimate illuminant color from neutral surfaces and check for yellow or amber shifts. Warm lighting shows higher red channel relative to blue in whites. Confirm that the cast is consistent across the frame. Avoid tagging when warmth comes from surface colors rather than light.
extraction_notes_3d: Use light source color temperature metadata where available. Tag when dominant lights have low color temperature values. Validate by sampling rendered white surfaces for warm bias.

definition_short: Surfaces or furnishings show wear, damage, or degradation beyond normal use.
definition_long: Use when the space exhibits visible wear such as scuffs, peeling paint, broken fixtures, or degraded materials. The wear should be noticeable and affect the overall impression. Apply when deterioration is more than minor scuffs. Do not use for clean, well maintained interiors. This tag overlaps with visible_dirt/decay but focuses on wear and damage.
examples_positive: A hallway with peeling paint and chipped edges.
A room with torn upholstery and broken tiles.
examples_negative: A freshly renovated space with intact finishes.
A clean room with no visible damage.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for visible damage patterns such as chips, cracks, or fraying. Ensure that wear is noticeable across meaningful areas. Distinguish wear from dirt by focusing on physical damage. Avoid tagging if wear is minimal or localized.
extraction_notes_3d: Use defect detection or material condition metadata to identify damaged areas. Tag when damage coverage exceeds a threshold. Consider both structural surfaces and furniture integrity.

definition_short: Textiles or soft furnishings such as curtains, rugs, or fabric elements are visibly present.
definition_long: Use when fabric-based elements are clearly visible and contribute to the scene. This includes curtains, drapes, rugs, upholstered panels, or fabric decor. Apply when textiles are a noticeable feature, not just minor accents. Do not use for spaces with only hard finishes and no visible fabrics. This tag is about soft furnishing presence.
examples_positive: A living room with curtains and a large rug.
An office with fabric wall panels and upholstered seats.
examples_negative: A room with only metal and glass surfaces.
A space with no visible fabric elements.
extraction_notes_2d: Detect fabric textures and soft materials such as curtains, rugs, and upholstered elements. Look for draped forms and textile patterns. Ensure textiles are visible and contribute to the scene composition. Avoid tagging if fabric elements are tiny or out of focus.
extraction_notes_3d: Use material classifications for fabrics and soft furnishings. Tag when textile materials occupy noticeable visible area. Consider the number and size of fabric objects in the view.
